God does not overlook the small cruelties of nations, and Ezekiel 25 forces us to reckon with this unsettling truth. While Judah faces exile—the consequence of her own rebellion against the Lord—her neighbors respond not with compassion but with mockery and glee. The Ammonites laugh when Jerusalem's sanctuary is profaned. Moab sneers that Judah is no different from any other nation now. Edom thirsts for vengeance. The Philistines strike with malicious satisfaction, seizing an opportunity to destroy an ancient enemy. These are not the grand empires of Babylon or Egypt. They are smaller powers, and their sins seem almost petty beside the weight of Judah's unfaithfulness. Yet God speaks against them with the same force He uses against His own people. Their schadenfreude, their contempt, their opportunistic violence—all of it is recorded and will be answered. The Lord does not distinguish between the sins of the great and the sins of the small. He sees the heart that exults in another's suffering.

What arrests us in this chapter is the repeated refrain: "Then you will know that I am the Lord." God will bring judgment not primarily to satisfy His wrath but to teach these nations something about His character. Through their destruction, they will come to know who He is. This is not merely punishment inflicted. It is a revelation forced upon the earth itself. The nations will understand that the God of Israel is sovereign over all peoples, that He does not permit mockery of His people to stand unchallenged, and that He governs history according to a purpose none of them can thwart. Even in judgment, God is making Himself known. Even in the wreckage He leaves behind, His dominion becomes visible.

There is a sobering warning here for any who are tempted to mock the afflicted or take pleasure in the fall of God's people. The exile of Judah is real and deserved, but that reality does not license her enemies to celebrate. There is a difference between accepting God's judgment and rejoicing in it with contempt. God's wrath falls on those who blur that line. He will not permit the nations to treat His people's suffering as entertainment or opportunity. And though this chapter speaks to ancient kingdoms now long forgotten, the principle endures: the God who saw the Ammonites' "Aha!" and the Philistines' malice sees still. He knows our hearts. He marks how we respond when others fall. We do well to examine ourselves honestly—not with fear, but with reverence—asking whether our words and attitudes toward those in distress reflect the character of the God we claim to follow.
